preamble.summary: Psalm 111 is an acrostic hymn of praise in which each half-line begins with the next letter of the Hebrew alphabet, moving from aleph to tav across twenty-two units. The psalmist praises the LORD in the assembly of the upright for His mighty works: His covenant faithfulness, His provision of food, His gift of the land, His justice, His trustworthy commands, and His redemption of His people. The psalm climaxes with the declaration that the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.
preamble.remarkable: The acrostic structure is the psalm's most striking formal feature — the poet praises God within the constraint of alphabetic order, as if declaring that the LORD's works fill every letter of the language. Psalm 111 and Psalm 112 form a matched pair: 111 describes who God is and what He does; 112 describes the person who fears God and mirrors those divine qualities. The vocabulary deliberately echoes between the two psalms — the words used for God's character in 111 reappear as the character of the righteous person in 112. The phrase 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (v. 10) connects this psalm to the wisdom tradition (Proverbs 1:7, 9:10, Job 28:28) and positions praise as a form of knowing.
preamble.friction: The Hebrew acrostic structure is impossible to reproduce in English without distorting the content. Each line of the psalm is extremely compressed — the poet is working under severe formal constraints, and the result reads more like a catalog of divine attributes than a flowing poem. Some scholars debate whether verse 1 is part of the acrostic (the opening Hallelujah stands outside the twenty-two-line count) or whether the acrostic begins with the first word after the Hallelujah. The WLC treats the Hallelujah as part of the verse text.
preamble.connections: Psalm 111 is the first half of an acrostic diptych with Psalm 112. Together they form a theological statement: the God described in 111 produces the person described in 112. The wisdom conclusion in verse 10 links to Proverbs 1:7 and 9:10. The covenant and redemption language (vv. 5, 9) connects to the Exodus and Sinai traditions. The psalm is liturgically associated with the Egyptian Hallel collection (Psalms 113-118) that follows immediately.

verses.text_hebrew: הַ֥לְלוּ יָ֨הּ ׀ אוֹדֶ֣ה יְ֭הוָה בְּכָל־לֵבָ֑ב בְּס֖וֹד יְשָׁרִ֣ים וְעֵדָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Praise ye the LORD. I will praise the LORD with my whole heart, in the assembly of the upright, and in the congregation.
verses.rendering:
Praise the LORD!
I will thank the LORD with all my heart,
in the council of the upright and in the congregation.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with Hallelujah — the liturgical call to praise — then shifts to first person: 'I will thank the LORD with all my heart.' The word odeh ('I will thank, give thanks') is the verb of public acknowledgment, not merely private gratitude. The setting is communal: besod yesharim ve-edah ('in the council of the upright and the congregation'). The word sod means 'intimate council, confidential gathering,' while edah is the broader assembly. The psalmist speaks to those who share the covenant commitment.
translator_notes: The acrostic begins here: aleph (odeh) and bet (besod) are the first two letters. Each half-verse advances one letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The word sod ('council, intimate circle') suggests a smaller gathering within the larger edah ('congregation'). The phrase bekhol levav ('with all my heart') uses levav rather than lev — the two forms are interchangeable in meaning, though levav appears more commonly in Deuteronomic and poetic texts.

key_terms.note: edah is one of several Hebrew words for gathered Israel. It emphasizes the idea of a community assembled by appointment or covenant obligation rather than casual gathering.

verses.text_kjv: The works of the LORD are great, sought out of all them that have pleasure therein.
verses.rendering:
Great are the works of the LORD,
studied by all who delight in them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The adjective gedolim ('great') modifies ma'asei YHWH ('the works of the LORD') — His acts in creation and history. The second line says these works are derushim ('sought out, studied, investigated') by all who have chefetz ('delight, pleasure') in them. The verb darash means to inquire, investigate, seek — it is the same verb used for consulting a prophet or searching the Torah. The psalm claims that God's works reward investigation: they are not opaque but yield meaning to those who approach them with delight rather than indifference.
translator_notes: The gimel and dalet lines of the acrostic. The participle derushim ('sought, studied') implies ongoing investigation, not a single act of observation. The phrase lekhol cheftzehem ('by all who delight in them') could also be read as 'according to all their desires' — the works of the LORD meet every inquiry.

verses.text_kjv: His work is honourable and glorious: and his righteousness endureth for ever.
verses.rendering:
Splendor and majesty mark His work,
and His righteousness stands forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: Hod ve-hadar ('splendor and majesty') is a paired expression found frequently in praise contexts — these words describe the visible glory that radiates from God's activity. His po'olo ('His work, His deed') carries this radiance. The second line declares that His tzidqato ('His righteousness') omedet la'ad ('stands forever'). The verb amad ('to stand') suggests permanence and reliability: God's righteousness is not temporary, not conditional, not subject to erosion.
translator_notes: The he and vav lines. The pair hod ve-hadar appears together in Psalm 104:1, 1 Chronicles 16:27, and Job 40:10, always describing divine or royal splendor. The noun po'olo ('His work') is singular — it refers to the totality of God's activity rather than to individual acts.

verses.text_hebrew: זֵ֣כֶר עָ֭שָׂה לְנִפְלְאֹתָ֑יו חַנּ֖וּן וְרַח֣וּם יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath made his wonderful works to be remembered: the LORD is gracious and full of compassion.
verses.rendering:
He has made a memorial of His wonders;
the LORD is gracious and compassionate.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word zekher ('memorial, remembrance') indicates that God has established ways for His nifle'otav ('His wonders') to be remembered — through festivals, through Torah, through liturgy. The wonders do not fade because God Himself has provided the mechanism of memory. The second line is a direct quotation of the divine self-disclosure from Exodus 34:6: channun ve-rachum YHWH ('gracious and compassionate is the LORD'). This creedal formula anchors the psalm's praise in the covenant revelation at Sinai.
translator_notes: The zayin and chet lines. The phrase channun ve-rachum ('gracious and compassionate') reverses the usual Exodus 34:6 order (rachum ve-channun) — likely for acrostic purposes, since chet must come after zayin. The reversal is not theologically significant but shows the poet adapting traditional language to the alphabetic constraint.

key_terms.semantic_range: gracious, showing favor, inclined to give generously
key_terms.note: channun derives from the root chanan ('to be gracious, to show favor'). It describes an unearned generosity — the disposition of someone who gives not because the recipient deserves it but because the giver is inclined toward generosity.

verses.text_hebrew: טֶ֭רֶף נָתַ֣ן לִירֵאָ֑יו יִזְכֹּ֖ר לְעוֹלָ֣ם בְּרִיתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath given meat unto them that fear him: he will ever be mindful of his covenant.
verses.rendering:
He gives food to those who fear Him;
He remembers His covenant forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: Teref ('food, prey, provision') is given to those who fear Him — the word teref carries the sense of food obtained, provision secured. God feeds His people as a direct expression of covenant loyalty. The second line states the reason: yizkor le'olam berito ('He remembers His covenant forever'). The verb zakhar ('to remember') when applied to God does not mean He recalls something forgotten — it means He acts on the basis of His commitment. God's remembering is God's doing.
translator_notes: The tet and yod lines. The word teref literally means 'torn food, prey' — what an animal brings back after hunting. Applied to God, it means provision. Some scholars connect this to the manna and quail in the wilderness; others see it as a general statement about divine provision. The covenant (berit) here is most likely the Sinai covenant, though the psalmist may also have the Abrahamic land promise in view given verse 6.

key_terms.note: berit is the foundational relational term of the Hebrew Bible — a solemn, binding commitment between parties. Here it refers to God's covenant with Israel, which is the basis for His ongoing provision and faithfulness.

verses.text_hebrew: כֹּ֣חַ מַ֭עֲשָׂיו הִגִּ֣יד לְעַמּ֑וֹ לָתֵ֥ת לָ֝הֶ֗ם נַחֲלַ֥ת גּוֹיִֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath shewed his people the power of his works, that he may give them the heritage of the heathen.
verses.rendering:
He declared the power of His works to His people,
giving them the inheritance of the nations.
verses.expanded_rendering: God revealed the ko'ach ('power, strength') of His deeds to His people — not in theory but in action, through the Exodus and conquest. The purpose was latet lahem nachalat goyim ('to give them the inheritance of the nations') — the land of Canaan, previously held by other peoples. The word nachalah ('inheritance') is the permanent, God-given portion — land as covenant gift, not as spoils of war.
translator_notes: The kaf and lamed lines. The 'inheritance of the nations' refers to the land of Canaan, which God allocated to Israel as promised to Abraham. The verb higgid ('He declared, made known') implies revelation — God showed His people what He could do so they would trust Him for what He was about to do.

verses.text_hebrew: מַעֲשֵׂ֣י יָ֭דָיו אֱמֶ֣ת וּמִשְׁפָּ֑ט נֶ֝אֱמָנִ֗ים כָּל־פִּקּוּדָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: The works of his hands are verity and judgment; all his commandments are sure.
verses.rendering:
The works of His hands are truth and justice;
all His precepts are trustworthy.
verses.expanded_rendering: Ma'asei yadav ('the works of His hands') are characterized by emet ('truth, reliability') and mishpat ('justice, right judgment'). These are not abstract qualities but descriptions of how God actually acts. The parallel line affirms that all His piqqudav ('His precepts, His charges') are ne'emanim ('trustworthy, faithful, reliable'). The adjective ne'emanim shares a root with emunah ('faithfulness') and amen — God's commands can be relied upon because God Himself is reliable.
translator_notes: The mem and nun lines. The noun piqqudim ('precepts, instructions') is relatively rare outside the Psalms and refers to God's detailed instructions or charges. The adjective ne'emanim ('trustworthy') comes from the root aman, which also produces emunah ('faithfulness') and amen ('so be it, it is reliable').

key_terms.note: emet means far more than factual accuracy — it describes something that is firm, stable, and dependable. When applied to God's works, it means they are solid, consistent, and can bear weight.

verses.text_hebrew: סְמוּכִ֣ים לָעַ֣ד לְעוֹלָ֑ם עֲ֝שׂוּיִ֗ם בֶּאֱמֶ֥ת וְיָשָֽׁר׃
verses.text_kjv: They stand fast for ever and ever, and are done in truth and uprightness.
verses.rendering:
They are established forever and ever,
carried out in truth and uprightness.
translator_notes: The samekh and ayin lines. The adjective semukhim ('established, supported, upheld') comes from the root samakh ('to lean on, to support') — God's precepts are stable, something a person can lean on without collapse. The phrase be'emet ve-yashar ('in truth and uprightness') describes the manner of their execution: straight, without crookedness or deception.

verses.text_hebrew: פְּד֤וּת ׀ שָׁ֘לַ֤ח לְעַמּ֗וֹ צִוָּֽה־לְעוֹלָ֥ם בְּרִית֑וֹ קָד֖וֹשׁ וְנוֹרָ֣א שְׁמֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He sent redemption unto his people: he hath commanded his covenant for ever: holy and reverend is his name.
verses.expanded_rendering: Pedut ('redemption, ransom') was sent to His people — the word implies liberation through payment of a price, rescue from bondage. This echoes the Exodus above all. He tsivvah ('commanded, established with authority') His berit ('covenant') le'olam ('forever'). The covenant is not a suggestion but a command — it has the force of divine decree. The psalm concludes this section by naming God as qadosh ve-nora ('holy and awe-inspiring'). His shemo ('His name') — His revealed identity — evokes reverence.
translator_notes: The pe, tsade, and qof lines. The verb tsivvah ('He commanded') applied to the covenant is unusual — normally one 'cuts' (karat) a covenant. Using tsivvah emphasizes that the covenant is an authoritative act of the divine will, not a negotiation. The adjective nora ('awe-inspiring, fearsome') is rendered here as 'awe-inspiring' rather than 'terrible' or 'dreadful' to capture the reverence rather than terror.

key_terms.note: pedut is the noun form of padah ('to redeem, to ransom'). It refers to liberation that costs something — not a free escape but a purchased freedom. In the Psalms it typically points to God's rescue of Israel from Egypt and from subsequent oppressions.

verses.text_hebrew: רֵ֘אשִׁ֤ית חָכְמָ֨ה ׀ יִרְאַ֬ת יְהוָ֗ה שֵׂ֣כֶל ט֭וֹב לְכָל־עֹשֵׂיהֶ֑ם תְּ֝הִלָּת֗וֹ עֹמֶ֥דֶת לָעַֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom: a good understanding have all they that do his commandments: his praise endureth for ever.
verses.expanded_rendering: Reshit chokhmah yir'at YHWH ('the beginning of wisdom is the fear of the LORD') is one of the most quoted sentences in the Hebrew Bible. Reshit ('beginning, first principle, starting point') does not mean merely the chronological start but the foundation, the controlling principle. Wisdom begins with yir'ah ('fear, reverence, awe') of the LORD — not terror but the recognition that God is God and the human is not. The second line affirms that sekhel tov ('good understanding, sound insight') belongs to all who oseh ('practice, do') the commandments. Wisdom is not passive knowledge but active obedience. The psalm closes where it began: tehillato omedet la'ad ('His praise endures forever').
translator_notes: The resh, shin, and tav lines complete the acrostic. The phrase reshit chokhmah parallels Proverbs 1:7 (reshit da'at) and Proverbs 9:10 (techillat chokhmah). The pronoun in lekhol oseihem ('all who do them') is ambiguous — 'them' could refer to the commandments or to the acts of wisdom. Most interpreters take it as referring to the commandments. The final line, tehillato omedet la'ad, mirrors verse 3b (tzidqato omedet la'ad), creating a frame: God's righteousness endures, and so does His praise.

key_terms.note: yir'at YHWH ('fear of the LORD') is the foundational posture of the wisdom tradition. It is not cringing terror but the recognition of God's supreme authority, holiness, and power — the awareness that shapes all other knowing.
